steady state.short: The level where a variable stops changing over time. In Solow, where investment exactly offsets depreciation + dilution, so k̇ = 0.

depreciation.short: The fraction of the capital stock that wears out each period, denoted δ.

savings rate.short: The constant fraction s of output that households save and firms invest each period.

capital per effective worker.short: Capital divided by labor measured in efficiency units, k̃ = K/(AL). The variable that converges in Solow.

diminishing returns.short: Each additional unit of capital adds less output than the last — the force that pulls the economy to a steady state.

total factor productivity.short: The efficiency term A multiplying the production function; its growth rate g sets long-run per-capita growth.

intertemporal budget constraint.short: All (c₁,c₂) bundles whose present value equals lifetime wealth: c₁ + c₂/(1+r) = W.

Euler equation.short: The optimality condition for consumption over time: marginal utility today equals (1+r)·β·marginal utility tomorrow. Here: c₂ = β(1+r)c₁.

consumption smoothing.short: The tendency of forward-looking agents to even out consumption across periods rather than match it to income timing.

Taylor rule.short: A monetary-policy rule: the central bank raises the real rate above neutral when inflation exceeds target — rₜ = r̄ + φπ(πₜ₋₁ − π*).

Taylor principle.short: For stability the policy response must be more than one-for-one with inflation (φπ > 1); otherwise inflation gaps are self-reinforcing.

output gap.short: The deviation of output from potential, Y − Ȳ. Positive gaps push inflation up via the Phillips curve.

expectations-augmented Phillips curve.short: Inflation equals expected inflation plus a term in the output gap: π = πᵉ + λ(Y − Ȳ).

expenditure approach.short: Measuring GDP as total final spending: Y = C + I + G + NX.

nominal GDP.short: Output valued at current prices — price and quantity changes mixed together.

real GDP.short: Output valued at constant prices, so changes reflect quantities rather than prices.

GDP deflator.short: The ratio of nominal to real GDP (×100) — the economy-wide price index implied by the accounts.

chain-weighting.short: Updating price weights every period and chaining the growth rates — the compromise between base-year choices used in official statistics.

purchasing power parity.short: Valuing every country's output at one common set of prices, so cross-country comparisons reflect quantities.
